summary: DHH argues against the growing trend of fundraising for open source projects, warning that introducing money corrupts the social dynamics that make open source successful. He contends that financial compensation causes work to expand unnecessarily (Parkinson's law), diminishes intrinsic motivation, and transforms peer collaboration into transactional buyer-seller relationships. He points to Ruby on Rails as proof that unpaid collaboration can produce results worth hundreds of millions of dollars. His core message is that open source thrives precisely because it operates outside market norms, and monetization threatens to destroy that ecosystem.
key_points: Financial constraints paradoxically make open source better — unpaid developers solve problems simply rather than embellishing work to justify funding
Introducing money transforms social gratitude into market expectations, killing the intrinsic motivation that drives open source contribution
Selective funding of some projects creates comparative worth signals that breed discontent among unfunded contributors
Ruby on Rails represents man-centuries of free labor worth hundreds of millions at market rates — proof that social norms outperform market norms
Once you cash in on open source goodwill through fundraising, that goodwill may be permanently spent
money_quotes: Once you've sold out, the goodwill might well be spent for good.
Once there is money involved, work will expand to fill the amount raised.
There is something lost when you share because you must, rather than because you can.
External, expected rewards diminish the intrinsic motivation of the fundraising open-source contributor. It risks transporting a community of peers into a transactional terminal.
We plant the seeds of discontent by selective monetary rewards.
Human nature is not so easily dismissed. We all hold an innate sense of justice, and that sense is disturbed when others take extra reward for similar work.
Open source has been such an incredible force for quality and community exactly because it's not been defined in market terms.
Like a coral reef, it's more sensitive than you think, and it's how to underestimate the beauty that's unwittingly at stake.
key_insight: Open source thrives on social norms and intrinsic motivation, and introducing market-based funding risks corrupting the very dynamics that make it superior to commercial software.
